news_summary: Recent breakthroughs reveal powerful methods to enhance mRNA therapy, halt cancer cell growth through vitamin discovery, prevent sepsis fatalities, and potentially build resilience against stress.
news_summary_fr: Des avancées récentes mettent en lumière des méthodes efficaces pour améliorer la thérapie à base d'ARNm, freiner la croissance des cellules cancéreuses grâce à la découverte d'une vitamine, prévenir les décès dus à la septicémie et, potentiellement, renforcer la résilience face au stress.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances revelan métodos eficaces para mejorar la terapia con ARNm, frenar el crecimiento de las células cancerosas gracias al descubrimiento de una vitamina, prevenir las muertes por sepsis y, potencialmente, desarrollar la capacidad de resistencia frente al estrés.

articles.title: This simple 3-amino acid trick boosts mRNA therapy 20-fold
articles.summary: A trio of common amino acids may hold the key to unlocking far more powerful gene therapies. Researchers found that adding them to lipid nanoparticles can boost mRNA delivery up to 20-fold and push CRISPR editing efficiency close to 90%. The trick isn’t changing the drug—but helping cells take it in more easily. In early tests, the approach dramatically improved survival and treatment outcomes, pointing to a simple but game-changing upgrade for future medicine.
articles.content:
Lipid nanoparticles, or LNPs, are best known for their role in delivering the COVID-19 mRNA vaccines given to billions of people. Now, scientists are expanding their use far beyond vaccines. Researchers are working to use these tiny carriers to deliver therapeutic mRNA into cells for cancer treatment, inflammatory diseases, and even CRISPR systems designed to fix harmful genetic mutations.

However, progress has been slowed by a persistent challenge. For LNPs to work inside the body, they must merge with cell membranes and release their cargo. While this process works efficiently in laboratory experiments, it is far less effective in real biological conditions.

Scientists Discover a Simple Amino Acid Solution

A research team at Biohub has identified an unexpectedly straightforward way to improve this process. In a study published in Science Translational Medicine, researchers led by Daniel Zongjie Wang, PhD, and Shana O. Kelley, Ph.D., showed that adding three common amino acids -- methionine, arginine, and serine -- alongside LNPs can dramatically enhance performance. This combination increased mRNA delivery by up to 20-fold and raised CRISPR gene editing efficiency from about 25 percent to nearly 90 percent after a single dose.

"Gene editing and mRNA-based therapies will play increasing roles in the medicine of the future, but they require LNPs to reach and enter cells," said Kelley, president of bioengineering at Biohub and head of Biohub, Chicago, where scientists are decoding the inflammatory processes that drive a wide range of diseases. "Any LNP formulation being developed today could potentially benefit from our approach."

The finding stems from the team's broader strategy of studying biology under conditions that better reflect the human body. "That's exactly what led us here," said Wang, who leads Biohub's Spatiotemporal Omics Group. "By asking why LNPs perform so differently in the physiological milieu of the body, we found a surprisingly simple answer that could make a wide range of mRNA and gene editing therapies substantially more effective."

A Metabolic Barrier Inside Cells

Until now, most efforts to improve LNP performance have focused on redesigning the nanoparticles themselves. Scientists have tested hundreds of new lipid combinations and used artificial intelligence to explore countless formulations. Despite these efforts, clinical results have remained underwhelming.

The Biohub researchers took a different approach. Instead of modifying the delivery system, they investigated whether the cells themselves were limiting uptake. They explored whether it might be possible to encourage cells to more readily absorb LNPs.

"The field has spent enormous effort engineering nanoparticles," said Wang. "We found, however, that the cell's own metabolic state is an equally important -- and addressable -- part of the equation."

Their work revealed that metabolism plays a key role. Standard lab-grown cells are exposed to nutrient-rich conditions that differ significantly from those inside the human body. When the team grew cells in a medium that more closely resembles human blood plasma, LNP uptake dropped sharply by 50 to 80 percent.

Further analysis showed that several amino acid-related metabolic pathways were less active under these more realistic conditions. This suggests that cells in the body operate with fewer available nutrients, which limits their ability to absorb nanoparticles.

A Simple Fix With Powerful Effects

To address this limitation, the researchers developed a targeted supplement containing methionine, arginine, and serine. When this mixture was given together with LNPs, the results were striking. Protein production from delivered mRNA increased between 5- and 20-fold across multiple cell types, both in laboratory experiments and in living animals.

The improvement held true across different delivery methods -- intramuscular, intratracheal, and intravenous -- and worked regardless of the specific nanoparticle design or genetic material being delivered. Additional studies showed that the amino acids enhance a cellular pathway that allows nanoparticles to enter cells more efficiently.

Dramatic Improvements in Animal Studies

The team tested the approach in disease models using both mRNA therapies and CRISPR gene editing.

In a mouse model of acetaminophen-induced acute liver failure -- the leading cause of drug-induced liver failure in human patients -- mice treated with growth hormone mRNA delivered by LNPs alone had a survival rate of just 33 percent. When the same treatment was combined with the amino acid supplement, survival rose to 100 percent. Levels of the therapeutic protein increased nearly ninefold, while indicators of liver damage and inflammation dropped to near-normal levels.

In another experiment, the researchers delivered CRISPR-Cas9 components to mouse lungs. Without the supplement, gene editing efficiency ranged from 20 to 30 percent. With the amino acid mixture, efficiency climbed to between 85 and 90 percent after a single dose. This level of improvement could be especially important for conditions such as cystic fibrosis, where effective gene correction in lung tissue is essential.

A Practical Path Toward Clinical Use

One of the most promising aspects of this discovery is how easy it may be to apply in real-world treatments. The supplement uses amino acids that are already produced at large scale and are considered safe. Unlike other strategies that require altering the nanoparticles or genetically modifying cells, this approach could simply be added to existing formulations.

By focusing on the biology of the cell rather than redesigning the delivery system, researchers may have found a practical way to unlock the full potential of mRNA therapies and gene editing technologies.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough that dramatically improves mRNA therapy and CRISPR gene editing efficiency by up to 20-fold and nearly 90%, respectively, using a simple amino acid supplement. This advancement has broad implications for future medicine, including treatments for genetic diseases, cancer, and acute liver failure, with demonstrated improved survival in animal models. The discovery is practical, scalable, and can enhance existing therapies, representing a meaningful positive impact on public health.
articles.personality_title: Three amino acids make gene therapies work much better
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists use tiny carriers called lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) to deliver mRNA medicines and gene-editing tools like CRISPR into cells. These carriers helped create COVID-19 vaccines but have struggled to work well inside real bodies because cells don’t absorb them easily.

**What happened** – Researchers at Biohub found that adding three common amino acids—methionine, arginine, and serine—along with LNPs can boost the delivery of mRNA by up to 20 times. This also increased CRISPR gene editing success from about 25% to nearly 90% in mice. The amino acids help cells take in the therapy more effectively, without changing the medicine itself.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it solves a major problem in gene therapy by focusing on how cells absorb treatments, not just on the carriers. In tests, mice with liver failure lived much longer when treated with this amino acid boost. The method works across different delivery styles and could improve many treatments for diseases like cancer and genetic disorders.

**What's next step** – Since the amino acids are safe and easy to produce, scientists plan to add this supplement to current gene therapies and test it in human trials. This could make future mRNA and gene-editing medicines much more effective and easier to develop.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Adding three simple amino acids to gene therapy carriers helps cells absorb treatments up to 20 times better, greatly improving their success in animals and paving the way for stronger medicines.
articles.personality_title_fr: Trois acides aminés améliorent nettement les thérapies géniques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques utilisent de minuscules transporteurs appelés nanoparticules lipidiques (LNP) pour délivrer des médicaments à base d’ARNm et des outils d’édition génétique comme CRISPR dans les cellules. Ces transporteurs ont aidé à créer les vaccins contre la COVID-19 mais fonctionnent mal dans le corps car les cellules les absorbent difficilement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Biohub ont découvert que l’ajout de trois acides aminés courants—méthionine, arginine et sérine—avec les LNP peut multiplier par 20 la livraison d’ARNm. Cela a aussi augmenté le succès de l’édition génétique CRISPR de 25 % à presque 90 % chez la souris. Ces acides aminés aident les cellules à mieux absorber la thérapie, sans changer le médicament.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle résout un problème majeur en thérapie génique en se concentrant sur la manière dont les cellules absorbent les traitements, et pas seulement sur les transporteurs. Dans les tests, les souris atteintes d’une insuffisance hépatique ont survécu beaucoup plus longtemps grâce à ce complément. La méthode marche avec différentes façons d’administrer les traitements et pourrait améliorer plusieurs thérapies contre le cancer et les maladies génétiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Comme ces acides aminés sont sûrs et faciles à produire, les scientifiques prévoient d’ajouter ce supplément aux thérapies actuelles et de le tester chez l’humain. Cela pourrait rendre les futurs médicaments à base d’ARNm et l’édition génétique beaucoup plus efficaces et simples à développer.

**Une phrase clé** – Ajouter trois acides aminés simples aux transporteurs de thérapie génique aide les cellules à absorber les traitements jusqu’à 20 fois mieux, améliorant grandement leur efficacité chez les animaux et ouvrant la voie à des médicaments plus puissants.
articles.personality_title_es: Tres aminoácidos mejoran mucho las terapias génicas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos usan pequeñas partículas llamadas nanopartículas lipídicas (LNP) para entregar medicamentos de ARNm y herramientas de edición genética como CRISPR dentro de las células. Estas partículas ayudaron a crear vacunas contra el COVID-19, pero funcionan mal en el cuerpo porque las células no las absorben bien.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Biohub descubrieron que agregar tres aminoácidos comunes—metionina, arginina y serina—a las LNP puede aumentar hasta 20 veces la entrega de ARNm. También mejoró la edición genética con CRISPR de un 25 % a casi un 90 % en ratones. Estos aminoácidos ayudan a las células a absorber mejor la terapia, sin cambiar el medicamento.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque resuelve un gran problema en las terapias génicas al enfocarse en cómo las células absorben los tratamientos, no solo en las partículas que los llevan. En pruebas, los ratones con insuficiencia hepática vivieron mucho más tiempo con este suplemento. El método funciona con diferentes formas de administrar y podría mejorar tratamientos para cáncer y enfermedades genéticas.

**Próximo paso** – Como los aminoácidos son seguros y fáciles de producir, los científicos planean añadir este suplemento a las terapias actuales y probarlo en humanos. Esto podría hacer que las medicinas de ARNm y edición genética sean mucho más efectivas y fáciles de desarrollar.

**Una frase clave** – Añadir tres aminoácidos simples a las partículas de terapia génica ayuda a las células a absorber los tratamientos hasta 20 veces mejor, mejorando mucho su éxito en animales y abriendo camino a medicinas más fuertes.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing cluster of three distinct, stylized amino acid molecules—methionine, arginine, and serine—intertwined with gently floating, softly illuminated lipid nanoparticles shaped like delicate spheres, all seamlessly merging into a translucent, abstract cell membrane that radiates vibrant energy and life, rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft pastels.

articles.title: This missing vitamin could stop cancer cells in their tracks
articles.summary: Cancer cells are known for their “glutamine addiction,” but many can escape this weakness by switching to alternative fuels. Researchers found that vitamin B7 acts like a metabolic “license,” enabling this escape route through a key enzyme. Without biotin, cancer cells lose that flexibility and stop growing. Mutations in a cancer-linked gene can make this vulnerability even stronger, offering a promising new target for therapy.
articles.content:
Researchers at the University of Lausanne (Unil) have uncovered a new biological mechanism that exposes a critical vulnerability in tumor cells when they are deprived of vitamin B7.

All cells must adjust to changes in nutrient supply to survive. However, some cells become especially reliant on glutamine, an amino acid that plays a major role in metabolism. Glutamine provides essential components needed to build proteins and DNA, and without it, cells cannot continue to grow and divide.

Cancer cells are a prime example. Many tumors exhibit what scientists call "glutamine addiction," meaning they depend heavily on this nutrient. Although this dependency is considered a weakness, many cancers find ways to work around it. In a study published in the journal Molecular Cell, a team led by Alexis Jourdain, assistant professor in the Department of Immunobiology (DIB) at Unil's Faculty of Biology and Medicine (FBM), sheds new light on the cellular processes behind this adaptability.

How Pyruvate and Vitamin B7 Keep Cells Growing

The research, led by Dr. Miriam Lisci, a postdoctoral scientist in Prof. Jourdain's lab, focused on carbon-rich molecules, especially pyruvate. These molecules can allow cells to keep dividing even when glutamine is scarce.

The team found that this process depends on a mitochondrial enzyme called pyruvate carboxylase. This enzyme requires vitamin B7 (or biotin) to function. When vitamin B7 is not available, the enzyme stops working, and cell growth comes to a halt. In this way, biotin acts as a "metabolic license," allowing pyruvate to feed into the cell's energy system and compensate for the lack of glutamine.

FBXW7 Gene Mutations Increase Cancer Vulnerability

The study also uncovered a new role for FBXW7, a gene frequently linked to cancer. "When FBXW7 is mutated -- a situation that is frequent in certain cancers -- pyruvate carboxylase partially disappears, pyruvate can no longer be used efficiently, and cells become dependent on glutamine," explains Miriam Lisci, first author of the article.

The researchers demonstrated that specific FBXW7 mutations found in patients can directly trigger this increased dependence on glutamine. These findings were made possible through collaborations with the FBM's metabolomics and proteomics platforms, as well as with Prof. Owen Skinner's team at Northeastern University in the United States.

Why Some Cancer Treatments Fail and What Comes Next

The findings also help explain why therapies aimed at blocking glutamine do not always succeed. Cancer cells can switch to alternative metabolic pathways to survive.

"In the longer term, this research opens up new avenues for better understanding the metabolic vulnerabilities of cancers and for designing innovative therapeutic strategies that take into account the great metabolic flexibility of tumor cells, notably by targeting several metabolic pathways simultaneously," concludes Alexis Jourdain, senior author of the study.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery revealing a new vulnerability in cancer cells related to vitamin B7, which could lead to innovative cancer therapies. This breakthrough has broad implications for cancer treatment, addressing a major health challenge with potential large-scale benefits. The article provides detailed context on the biological mechanism, gene mutations involved, and therapeutic implications, meeting the criteria for substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: Vitamin B7 shortage stops cancer cells from growing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cancer cells need a nutrient called glutamine to grow. But some cancer cells can survive even when glutamine is low by using other fuels.

**What happened** – Scientists at the University of Lausanne discovered that vitamin B7, also called biotin, helps cancer cells switch to another fuel called pyruvate. This switch depends on an enzyme that needs vitamin B7 to work. Without vitamin B7, the enzyme stops, and cancer cells stop growing. They also found that changes in a gene called FBXW7 make some cancer cells even more dependent on glutamine.

**Impact** – This discovery explains why some cancer treatments that block glutamine don’t always work. It shows that vitamin B7 is crucial for cancer cells to adapt and keep growing. Targeting vitamin B7 or the related enzyme could help create new cancer treatments that block cancer cells from using different fuels.

**What's next step** – Researchers will explore how to use this knowledge to develop drugs that target these metabolic pathways. They hope to create therapies that block cancer cells’ ability to switch fuels, making treatments more effective.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Without vitamin B7, cancer cells lose their ability to adapt and grow, revealing a new target for cancer treatment.
articles.personality_title_fr: La carence en vitamine B7 bloque la croissance des cellules cancéreuses
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les cellules cancéreuses ont besoin d’un nutriment appelé glutamine pour grandir. Mais certaines cellules cancéreuses peuvent survivre même lorsque la glutamine est faible en utilisant d’autres sources d’énergie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université de Lausanne ont découvert que la vitamine B7, aussi appelée biotine, aide les cellules cancéreuses à utiliser un autre carburant appelé pyruvate. Ce changement dépend d’une enzyme qui a besoin de vitamine B7 pour fonctionner. Sans vitamine B7, l’enzyme s’arrête et les cellules cancéreuses cessent de grandir. Ils ont aussi trouvé que des mutations dans un gène nommé FBXW7 rendent certaines cellules cancéreuses encore plus dépendantes de la glutamine.

**Impact** – Cette découverte explique pourquoi certains traitements contre le cancer qui bloquent la glutamine ne fonctionnent pas toujours. Elle montre que la vitamine B7 est essentielle pour que les cellules cancéreuses s’adaptent et continuent de grandir. Cibler la vitamine B7 ou l’enzyme liée pourrait aider à créer de nouveaux traitements contre le cancer qui empêchent les cellules d’utiliser différentes sources d’énergie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier comment utiliser cette découverte pour développer des médicaments qui bloquent ces voies métaboliques. Ils espèrent créer des traitements qui empêchent les cellules cancéreuses de changer de carburant, rendant les thérapies plus efficaces.

**Une phrase clé** – Sans vitamine B7, les cellules cancéreuses perdent leur capacité d’adaptation et de croissance, révélant une nouvelle cible pour le traitement du cancer.
articles.personality_title_es: La falta de vitamina B7 detiene el crecimiento de las células cancerosas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las células cancerosas necesitan un nutriente llamado glutamina para crecer. Pero algunas células cancerosas pueden sobrevivir incluso cuando hay poca glutamina usando otras fuentes de energía.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de Lausana descubrieron que la vitamina B7, también llamada biotina, ayuda a las células cancerosas a usar otro combustible llamado piruvato. Este cambio depende de una enzima que necesita vitamina B7 para funcionar. Sin vitamina B7, la enzima se detiene y las células cancerosas dejan de crecer. También encontraron que mutaciones en un gen llamado FBXW7 hacen que algunas células cancerosas dependan aún más de la glutamina.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento explica por qué algunos tratamientos contra el cáncer que bloquean la glutamina no siempre funcionan. Muestra que la vitamina B7 es clave para que las células cancerosas se adapten y sigan creciendo. Apuntar a la vitamina B7 o a la enzima relacionada podría ayudar a crear nuevos tratamientos que impidan que las células usen diferentes combustibles.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores explorarán cómo usar este conocimiento para desarrollar medicamentos que bloqueen estas rutas metabólicas. Esperan crear terapias que impidan que las células cancerosas cambien de combustible, haciendo los tratamientos más efectivos.

**Frase clave** – Sin vitamina B7, las células cancerosas pierden su capacidad de adaptarse y crecer, mostrando un nuevo objetivo para el tratamiento del cáncer.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, intricate molecular structure shaped like a key (representing vitamin B7) unlocking a vibrant, branching cellular network where one branch is brightly lit with flowing pyruvate molecules, while another branch with a broken, dimmed enzyme symbol shows halted growth, all set against a soft, abstract background of interconnected metabolic pathways symbolizing cancer cells’ adaptive energy systems.

articles.title: A whole new way to prevent death from sepsis shows promise
articles.summary: Filtering a protein that may cause sepsis out from the blood has shown promising signs for improving survival
articles.content:
Filtering a protein that may cause sepsis out from the blood has shown promising signs for improving survival

Extracting a patient’s blood plasma, removing a particular protein and then reintroducing the liquid could improve sepsis outcomes Vital Hil/Shutterstock

People with severe sepsis could one day be treated by having their blood filtered to remove a crucial protein that seems to drive the life-threatening reaction. The approach has shown promise in animal models, with aims for a trial in people next year.

Sepsis occurs when the body’s immune system overreacts to an infection, damaging tissues and organs. It can also escalate into septic shock, in which blood pressure falls dramatically, leading to even more damage. In 2017, there were an estimated 49 million cases of sepsis worldwide. A later meta-analysis of patients in Europe, North America and Australia found that 32 per cent of people with sepsis die within 90 days, rising to 39 per cent for those with septic shock, despite treatments being available to treat the initial infection and compensate for organ damage.

But a novel approach that filters out a driver of this process could stop sepsis in its tracks. Isaac Eliaz at the Amitabha Medical Clinic and Healing Center in Santa Rosa, California, has spent decades studying a protein called galectin-3. It has many functions in healthy people, notably in regulating how cells grow, divide and die, and in activating some immune cells. Due to its multiple functions, galectin-3 has been implicated in many conditions. “It covers a whole array of diseases, from autoimmunity to cancer,” says Eliaz.

This led Eliaz to wonder if galectin-3 might contribute to sepsis. Several studies have shown that higher levels of galectin-3 are associated with a higher risk of mortality in people with sepsis.

So Eliaz and his colleagues developed a device to filter galectin-3 out of the blood. This involves inserting a line into a patient to drain a volume of blood, which is then placed in a centrifuge to separate the cells from the liquid plasma. The plasma is then passed through a filter that contains antibodies against galectin-3, selectively removing the protein. The liquid and cells are then recombined and returned to the patient.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

This apheresis device has now been tested by a team led by Zhiyong Peng at the Zhongnan Hospital of Wuhan University in China, using a three-pronged approach.

First, they tracked 87 people with sepsis and 27 healthy volunteers, and found that those with sepsis had higher levels of galectin-3. Those levels then declined among those who survived.

The team also tested the blood-filtration device in two animal models of sepsis. The first involved 48 rats that developed sepsis after having part of their large intestine punctured. Twenty-eight then had their blood filtered for galectin-3, while the remainder were given sham apheresis. Of the treatment group, 57 per cent survived, compared to just 25 per cent of the controls.

The researchers also tried galectin-3 apheresis in miniature pigs that were given lipopolysaccharide, a chemical in bacteria that triggers a powerful immune response, and thus sepsis. The pigs were treated using all the methods of an intensive care unit, but 16 were also given galectin-3 apheresis and 15 had sham apheresis. Again, the survival rate was higher in the treated group: 69 per cent compared to 27 per cent.

“This is innovative, for sure,” says Djillali Annane at the Raymond Poincaré Hospital in Garches, France. “The results are consistent in the two animal models.” However, he says there is a long way to go until galectin-3 apheresis could become standard practice, for instance, understanding how galectin-3 contributes to sepsis on a mechanistic level. Annane also wants the results to be replicated by independent groups, and in other animals, such as primates.

Eliaz’s company, Eliaz Therapeutics, is now trying to secure funding for a randomised clinical trial of galectin-3 apheresis in people, which it aims to carry out in 2027.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a novel medical approach that filters a protein linked to sepsis from the blood, showing promising survival improvements in animal models and plans for human trials. Sepsis is a major global health issue with high mortality, so this breakthrough has significant potential impact on public health. The article provides detailed context on the protein, the device, and experimental results, indicating substantial substance and broad relevance.
articles.personality_title: New blood filter shows promise in improving sepsis survival
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Sepsis is a serious condition where the body’s response to infection harms its own tissues and organs. It causes millions of deaths worldwide each year, despite current treatments. Researchers are looking for new ways to stop sepsis early and save lives.

**What happened** – Scientists developed a device that removes a protein called galectin-3 from the blood. This protein is linked to worse outcomes in sepsis. The device takes blood from a patient, filters out galectin-3, and returns the cleaned blood. Tests in rats and pigs with sepsis showed higher survival rates when treated with this filtration compared to those without it.

**Impact** – These results are important because filtering out galectin-3 could reduce the damage sepsis causes. In the animal tests, survival more than doubled in some cases. This method offers a new way to treat sepsis by targeting a specific cause inside the blood, not just fighting infection or supporting organs.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to start clinical trials in people by 2027 to see if the treatment works safely and effectively in humans. They also want other scientists to confirm their findings and better understand how galectin-3 causes harm during sepsis.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Filtering galectin-3 from blood shows promise in animal studies to improve sepsis survival and may lead to new treatments for patients in the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau filtre sanguin prometteur pour améliorer la survie au sepsis
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le sepsis est une maladie grave où la réponse du corps à une infection endommage ses propres tissus et organes. Il cause des millions de décès chaque année dans le monde, malgré les traitements actuels. Les chercheurs cherchent de nouvelles façons d’arrêter le sepsis tôt pour sauver des vies.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont développé un appareil qui retire une protéine appelée galectine-3 du sang. Cette protéine est liée à de pires résultats dans le sepsis. L’appareil prélève le sang, filtre la galectine-3, puis renvoie le sang nettoyé. Des tests sur des rats et des cochons atteints de sepsis ont montré une meilleure survie avec ce traitement qu’en son absence.

**Impact** – Ces résultats sont importants car retirer la galectine-3 pourrait réduire les dégâts causés par le sepsis. Dans les tests animaux, la survie a parfois plus que doublé. Cette méthode propose une nouvelle façon de traiter le sepsis en ciblant une cause précise dans le sang, pas seulement en combattant l’infection ou en soutenant les organes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de lancer des essais cliniques sur des humains d’ici 2027 pour vérifier si le traitement est sûr et efficace. Ils souhaitent aussi que d’autres scientifiques confirment leurs résultats et comprennent mieux comment la galectine-3 nuit pendant le sepsis.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Filtrer la galectine-3 du sang montre un potentiel encourageant pour améliorer la survie au sepsis chez les animaux et pourrait conduire à de nouveaux traitements pour les patients.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo filtro sanguíneo muestra promesa para mejorar la supervivencia al sepsis
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El sepsis es una condición grave donde la respuesta del cuerpo a una infección daña sus propios tejidos y órganos. Causa millones de muertes en todo el mundo cada año, a pesar de los tratamientos actuales. Los investigadores buscan nuevas formas de detener el sepsis temprano y salvar vidas.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos desarrollaron un dispositivo que elimina una proteína llamada galectina-3 de la sangre. Esta proteína está relacionada con peores resultados en el sepsis. El dispositivo toma la sangre, filtra la galectina-3 y devuelve la sangre limpia. Pruebas en ratas y cerdos con sepsis mostraron tasas de supervivencia más altas con este tratamiento que sin él.

**Impacto** – Estos resultados son importantes porque filtrar la galectina-3 podría reducir el daño que causa el sepsis. En las pruebas con animales, la supervivencia se duplicó o más en algunos casos. Este método ofrece una nueva forma de tratar el sepsis al atacar una causa específica en la sangre, no solo combatiendo la infección o apoyando los órganos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean iniciar ensayos clínicos en personas para 2027 para ver si el tratamiento es seguro y efectivo en humanos. También quieren que otros científicos confirmen sus hallazgos y entiendan mejor cómo la galectina-3 causa daño durante el sepsis.

**Resumen en una frase** – Filtrar la galectina-3 de la sangre muestra potencial en estudios con animales para mejorar la supervivencia al sepsis y podría llevar a nuevos tratamientos para pacientes en el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a gentle, glowing blood filtration device shaped like an elegant, transparent vessel with flowing red and clear plasma streams weaving through it, surrounded by soft, abstract representations of protective antibodies gently capturing and removing glowing protein particles, set against a calm, natural background of muted earth tones.

articles.title: Can we ‘vaccinate’ ourselves against stress?
articles.summary: A traditional vaccine primes the immune system to build better defences. Researchers think we can do something similar to increase our resilience to the pressures and worries of life
articles.content:
A traditional vaccine primes the immune system to build better defences. Researchers think we can do something similar to increase our resilience to the pressures and worries of life

Mojo Wang

It might sound strange to talk about inoculating ourselves against stress, but we might be able to do just that.

Just as a vaccine safely trains the immune system so it can defend against later attack, researchers think we can do something similar with stress.

This idea is often studied in military personnel. Exposing soldiers to simulations of stressful events, while giving them the tools and support to cope, can minimise the impact of future stress. In one study, cadets who received resilience training had lower cortisol levels in later intense military exercises than those who hadn’t received the training. Likewise, paramedics who have received resilience training are also at lower risk of developing post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and depression.

Fortunately, most people don’t need military-style training to gain these benefits. Deliberately exposing yourself to manageable, everyday stress can build resilience, says Julie Vašků at Masaryk University in the Czech Republic.

Recent research shows that successfully navigating stressful situations remodels the brain, provoking changes across the “stress network”, including the prefrontal cortex, which regulates emotions; the hippocampus, involved in memory; and the amygdala, which detects threats. When people voluntarily face mild stressors, these circuits adapt in ways that make future challenges easier to handle and help your body return to baseline more quickly.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

The key is that the stress must remain manageable. A mild stressor is a situation in which you feel some sort of bearable discomfort, says Vašků. “It cannot be overwhelming. Once it’s overwhelming, it’s traumatising.” She suggests going somewhere unfamiliar or speaking with people you wouldn’t normally approach. Additional support can help. “Bring someone else with you,” she says.

This kind of exposure therapy works well in adults, but should we apply it in childhood, too? Severe childhood adversity clearly increases the risk of later mental and physical health problems, yet small amounts of mild adversity may bring benefits. In rodents, continuous maternal separation from birth increases adult stress responses, whereas the same amount of maternal separation but given in small bursts produces more resilient adults. Similar patterns have been observed in primates exposed to short periods of maternal separation.

It is difficult to translate such findings to humans, and unethical to run comparable experiments. Still, Carmine Pariante at King’s College London suggests “we could be slightly less protective as a society”. By this, of course, he doesn’t mean deliberately exposing anyone to trauma, but occasionally allowing ourselves – and our children – to experience more manageable challenges.

Exposing soldiers to simulations of stressful events can help them build resilience for when they face such situations for real Daniel Ceng/Anadolu via Getty Images

Vašků points to a cultural example from the Czech Republic, where children often perform classical music from a young age. “We put 5-year-olds on stage with their teacher. A few years later, they perform with their friends. When they’re older, they’re on their own, but they have a teacher nearby.” By adolescence, they are able to perform totally alone, she says. They may still experience the same stress as a novice, but they are better equipped to cope and recover more quickly from the stress because of the early, controlled exposure.

Exposure isn’t the only way to build resilience. Techniques such as breathing exercises, mindfulness, learning to change your mindset about stressful situations and reviewing your strengths have all been shown to improve resilience and turn bad stress into good.

Some researchers are even exploring a literal stress vaccine. A number of studies in rodents show that vaccination with a bacterium called Mycobacterium vaccae that has been killed off by heat calms their stress response by inducing an anti-inflammatory effect. Even more experimental is research into drugs known as “alexigents”, which aim to enhance stress resilience in people at high risk of developing PTSD and depression. But so far, the only significant development is a 2017 study that showed a single dose of ketamine could protect mice from the negative effects of stress.

For most of us, the answer is simpler: stress isn’t the enemy (see “Why the right kind of stress is crucial for your health and happiness”). “You actually want to experience stress,” says Vašků. “You want to be able to react to it – it’s there for a reason – but you also want to be able to recover quickly. And building resilience is key to bringing your stress hormones back down to normal.”
articles.reasoning: The article discusses research-backed methods to build resilience against stress, including psychological training and potential biomedical approaches, which have broad implications for mental health and wellbeing. It provides detailed context on how manageable stress exposure can improve emotional regulation and reduce risks of disorders like PTSD and depression, benefiting society at large.
articles.personality_title: Training the brain to handle stress: new ways to build resilience
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Stress affects everyone, but scientists are exploring how to prepare our minds to handle it better, similar to how vaccines prepare the body to fight diseases.

**What happened** – Researchers have studied ways to expose people to mild, manageable stress to help them build resilience. For example, soldiers and paramedics who receive special training show lower stress hormone levels and fewer mental health problems later. Studies also show that facing small challenges can change brain areas that control emotions and memory, making future stress easier to manage.

**Impact** – This approach is important because it offers a natural way to protect mental health without needing medicine. It shows that not all stress is bad—mild stress can help us grow stronger. For example, children who perform music in front of others learn to handle stress early, helping them cope better as they get older.

**What's next step** – Scientists are testing new ideas like vaccines or drugs that might reduce stress reactions, but these are still in early stages. Meanwhile, people can build resilience by facing small challenges safely, practicing mindfulness, or learning to change how they think about stress.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Facing small, manageable stresses can train the brain to handle bigger challenges, helping us stay healthier and recover faster from pressure.
articles.personality_title_fr: Apprendre au cerveau à gérer le stress : de nouvelles méthodes pour renforcer la résilience
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le stress touche tout le monde, mais les scientifiques cherchent comment préparer notre esprit à mieux le gérer, un peu comme un vaccin prépare le corps à combattre les maladies.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié comment exposer les gens à un stress léger et contrôlé pour les aider à devenir plus résilients. Par exemple, les soldats et les ambulanciers formés montrent des niveaux plus faibles d’hormones du stress et moins de problèmes de santé mentale par la suite. Les études montrent aussi que faire face à de petits défis change certaines parties du cerveau qui contrôlent les émotions et la mémoire, ce qui facilite la gestion du stress futur.

**Impact** – Cette approche est importante car elle offre un moyen naturel de protéger la santé mentale sans médicament. Elle montre que tout stress n’est pas mauvais : un stress léger peut nous rendre plus forts. Par exemple, des enfants qui jouent de la musique en public apprennent à gérer le stress tôt, ce qui les aide plus tard.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques testent des vaccins ou médicaments qui pourraient réduire les réactions au stress, mais c’est encore au début. En attendant, chacun peut renforcer sa résilience en affrontant de petits défis en toute sécurité, en pratiquant la pleine conscience ou en changeant sa façon de penser le stress.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Affronter de petits stress maîtrisés peut entraîner le cerveau à gérer de plus grands défis, aidant à rester en meilleure santé et à récupérer plus vite du stress.
articles.personality_title_es: Entrenar el cerebro para manejar el estrés: nuevas formas de fortalecer la resiliencia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El estrés afecta a todos, pero los científicos están buscando cómo preparar nuestra mente para manejarlo mejor, parecido a cómo una vacuna prepara al cuerpo para combatir enfermedades.

**Qué pasó** – Los investigadores han estudiado cómo exponer a las personas a un estrés leve y controlado para ayudarles a ser más resilientes. Por ejemplo, soldados y paramédicos entrenados muestran niveles más bajos de hormonas del estrés y menos problemas de salud mental después. Los estudios también muestran que enfrentar pequeños desafíos cambia áreas del cerebro que controlan las emociones y la memoria, haciendo más fácil manejar el estrés futuro.

**Impacto** – Este método es importante porque ofrece una forma natural de proteger la salud mental sin necesidad de medicamentos. Demuestra que no todo estrés es malo: el estrés leve puede hacernos más fuertes. Por ejemplo, niños que tocan música en público aprenden a manejar el estrés desde pequeños, lo que les ayuda más adelante.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos están probando vacunas o medicamentos que podrían reducir las reacciones al estrés, pero aún están en etapas iniciales. Mientras tanto, las personas pueden construir resiliencia enfrentando pequeños retos con seguridad, practicando mindfulness o cambiando su forma de pensar sobre el estrés.

**Resumen en una frase** – Enfrentar pequeños estreses manejables puede entrenar al cerebro para afrontar retos mayores, ayudándonos a estar más saludables y recuperarnos más rápido del estrés.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy, glowing brain-shaped seedling growing from soil, surrounded by gentle waves of light symbolizing neural pathways adapting and strengthening, with soft silhouettes of a soldier’s helmet and a musical instrument subtly integrated into the background to represent resilience training and controlled exposure to manageable stress.
